units.title: Container Element (div)
units.summary: The HTML div element is used to define a container that groups other HTML elements together.
units.source_span:
The HTML `div` element defines a container. ```html

Tourism

Plan your trip wherever you want to go

```

explanation.text: The HTML `div` element is a container—a box that holds and groups other HTML elements together. You use it to organize related content like headings, paragraphs, and buttons into one unit, making it easier to style or manage them as a group.

analogy.text: A div is like a cardboard box that holds different items together. The box itself (the div) is the container, and the items inside it—a label, a description card, and a button—are like the h1, p, and button elements grouped within the div. Just as the box groups these separate items into one unit, the div groups these separate HTML elements into one container.
analogy.grounding_check: Box = 'container' (source: 'div element defines a container'); items inside (label, description, button) = h1, p, button elements shown in the source code example grouped within the div tags.

scenarios.text: You're building a tourism website and want to group a heading, description, and button together so they stay visually connected. You wrap all three inside a `div` to keep them organized as one section.

code_playground.html:
Tourism

Plan your trip wherever you want to go

questions.question: What is the main purpose of the HTML div element?
options: To define a container that groups other HTML elements together
To create a line break between elements
To add styling directly to text
To embed images in a webpage
questions.explanation: The source span explicitly states that the div element 'defines a container' that groups other HTML elements together. The other options describe different HTML elements or features, not the div's purpose.

questions.question: In the example, what elements are grouped inside the div container?
options: An h1 heading, a paragraph, and a button
Only the h1 heading and the button
Only the paragraph and the button
questions.explanation: The source span shows a div containing three elements: an h1 with 'Tourism', a p with 'Plan your trip wherever you want to go', and a button with 'Get Started'. All three are grouped inside the div.

questions.question: Which of the following is a valid use of a div element?
options: Grouping a heading, text, and a button together
Replacing the h1 element for all page headings
questions.explanation: The source span example shows a div grouping an h1, a paragraph, and a button together, demonstrating that this is a valid use. The other options either misrepresent the div's role or describe different elements.

questions.question: What type of element is the div?
questions.explanation: The source span explicitly identifies the div as a 'container' element. The other options describe different categories of HTML elements, not what a div is.

units.title: CSS Rule Syntax
units.summary: CSS rules consist of a selector followed by a block containing one or more property-value pairs.

explanation.text: A CSS rule is a block of styling instructions made up of two parts: a selector (which targets which elements to style) and a declaration block (the curly braces containing one or more property-value pairs). Each property-value pair tells the browser what style to apply—the property is the style name (like color or font-size) and the value is what you want it to be (like blue or 16px).

analogy.text: A CSS rule is like addressing a package: the selector is like writing the recipient's name on the envelope (specifying who gets it), and the curly braces contain the delivery instructions inside, just like property-value pairs (color: blue; font-size: 16px) are the specific details about how to style that selected element.
analogy.grounding_check: selector → 'selector followed by' in source; property-value pairs → 'block containing one or more property-value pairs' in source; curly braces → implied by the code block structure showing { } syntax

scenarios.text: You want to make all paragraph text blue and increase its size. You write a CSS rule with the selector `p`, then inside the braces add `color: blue;` and `font-size: 18px;` as two separate property-value pairs.

code_playground.html:
This paragraph is blue and larger.

This one too.

questions.question: What two main parts make up a CSS rule?
options: A selector and a declaration block
questions.explanation: The source shows a CSS rule has a selector (the part before the curly braces) and a block with property-value pairs inside curly braces. The other options name parts that exist *within* a rule or are unrelated CSS concepts.

questions.question: In a CSS rule, what goes inside the curly braces?
questions.explanation: The source span shows that inside the curly braces are property-value pairs separated by semicolons (like `property1: value1;`). The selector goes *before* the braces, not inside them.

questions.question: How should each property-value pair end in a CSS rule?
questions.explanation: The source shows each property-value pair ends with a semicolon (for example, `property1: value1;`). A colon separates the property from the value, but a semicolon terminates the pair.

questions.question: In the CSS rule shown, what is `property1: value1;` an example of?
questions.explanation: The source shows `property1: value1;` as one item inside the declaration block—this is a property-value pair. The selector is the part before the braces, and the declaration block is the entire curly-braced section.

units.title: Text Align Property
units.summary: The CSS text-align property controls the horizontal alignment of text within an HTML element using values like center, left, or right.
units.source_span:
The CSS `text-align` property specifies the horizontal alignment of the text in an HTML element.

```css
.h-center {
  text-align: center;
}
```

| Value  | Description                   |
| ------ | ----------------------------- |
| center | Aligns the text to the center |
| left   | Aligns the text to the left   |
| right  | Aligns the text to the right  |

explanation.text: The CSS `text-align` property controls where text lines up horizontally inside an element. You give it a value like `center`, `left`, or `right` to move all the text in that direction.

analogy.text: Imagine text as books on a shelf. The `text-align: left` value is like pushing all books to the left edge of the shelf, the `text-align: center` value is like stacking them in the middle, and the `text-align: right` value is like pushing them to the right edge. Just as each shelf position determines where the books sit horizontally, each text-align value determines where the text sits horizontally within the element.
analogy.grounding_check: left/center/right values trace to 'Aligns the text to the left/center/right'; horizontal positioning traces to 'specifies the horizontal alignment of the text in an HTML element'

scenarios.text: You want a website heading to appear in the middle of the page instead of on the left side where it normally sits.

code_playground.html:
Welcome to My Site

This paragraph stays left-aligned by default.

questions.question: What does the CSS text-align property control?
options: The horizontal alignment of text within an element
The vertical alignment of text within an element
The size of the text in an element
The color of the text in an element
questions.explanation: The source states that text-align specifies the horizontal alignment of text. Vertical alignment, size, and color are controlled by different CSS properties, not text-align.

questions.question: Which text-align value centers text horizontally?
questions.explanation: The source table lists 'center' as the value that aligns text to the center. 'middle' is used for vertical alignment in other properties; the other options are not valid text-align values.

questions.question: What happens when you set text-align to left?
options: Text aligns to the left side of the element
Text aligns to the right side of the element
Text moves to the left margin of the page
Text is indented from the left
questions.explanation: The source table states that the 'left' value aligns text to the left. The other options describe different effects unrelated to the text-align property's function.

questions.question: Which value would you use to align text to the right side of an element?
questions.explanation: The source table lists 'right' as the value that aligns text to the right. The other options are not valid text-align values; float and other properties handle different layout tasks.
